docs.title: Introduction
docs.text:
Let’s just get started with Genstack.

Genstack helps you plug AI directly into your workflow with zero fuss. This guide walks you through the basic setup so you’re not stuck fiddling with boilerplate.

docs.title: Setup Genstack
docs.text: Before using Genstack in code, you’ll need to activate your account and get your API key from the Genstack web app.

docs.title: Sign in to Genstack
docs.text: Head over to Genstack and sign in with your Google account or email.

docs.title: Create a Project
docs.text:
Once logged in:

  • Go to the Home tab

  • Click New Project

  • Give it a name and hit Create

Each project keeps its own API key and usage.

docs.title: Introduction to Tracks
docs.text:
Tracks exist within a project and give you complete control over:

  • Supported modalities
  • Customizable response formats
  • Selectable models via API

No rigid flows. You configure everything.

docs.title: Build Your Own Track on Genstack
docs.text: Follow these steps:

docs.title: 1. Name
docs.text: Give your track a short, clear name.

docs.title: 2. Track ID
docs.text: Auto-generated from the name, or define your own.

docs.title: 3. Description
docs.text: A quick summary to help you identify the track later.

docs.title: 4. Type
docs.text:
Select the model category: - Reasoning models - Standard LLMs

⚠️ This doesn’t affect model options—just how the track is classified.

docs.title: 5. Modalities
docs.text: Choose input/output types. Currently: text only (both directions)

docs.title: 6. Tools
docs.text:
Add tools your models can use: - Function calling - Web search

Your track. Your logic. Your control.

docs.title: Prerequisites
docs.text:
Before you proceed, make sure you have:

  • Python installed (version 3.10 or above is recommended)
  • pip — Python’s package manager (comes bundled with Python)

docs.title: Check your setup
docs.text:
To confirm you have Python and pip ready, run these in your terminal:

python --version
pip --version

If both commands show versions (like Python 3.11.5), you're good to go.

docs.title: (Optional) Use a virtual environment
docs.text: It’s a good idea to isolate dependencies per project. To create and activate a virtual environment:

docs.title: On macOS/Linux:
docs.text:
python -m venv venv
source venv/bin/activate

docs.title: On Windows:
docs.text:
python -m venv venv
venv\Scripts\activate

You’ll know it’s active when your terminal prompt starts with (venv).

docs.title: Install dependencies
docs.text:
Run the following command to install Genstack and its required packages:

pip install genstack python-dotenv

docs.title: Set up environment variables
docs.text:
Genstack uses environment variables to manage sensitive config like API keys.

Create a .env file in your project root and add the following:

GENSTACK_API_KEY=your-api-key-here

Replace your-api-key-here with your actual API key. This file should not be committed to version control (add it to your .gitignore).

docs.title: Run your first Genstack response
docs.text:
Here's a minimal example to make sure everything's wired up right:

#
from genstack import Genstack
from dotenv import load_dotenv
import os

#
load_dotenv()

#
client = Genstack(api_key=os.getenv("GENSTACK_API_KEY"))

#
response = client.generate(input="Are you ready?", model="<MODEL_ID>", track="<YOUR_TRACK_NAME>")

#
print(response)

If everything’s set up, you should see a response printed back from the AI. That’s it — you’re in.
